---
title: "Boreal Birds Dashboard"
author: "Harrison Li, Joel Peterson, Suryash Chakravarty, Wesley Beard"
date: "2024-05-07"
format:
   pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: Contents
    number-sections: true
---

## Executive Summary

The Boreal Avian Modelling Centre (BAM) requires a transition from static model visuals to a high-fidelity, interactive analytical platform. Current version 4 outputs are restricted to simple data tables and static summaries, that fail to leverage the depth of the underlying machine learning models. Version 5 models introduce improved raster predictions, spatially explicit density estimates, and habitat relationship metrics across 144 species. 

The proposed dashboard will convert 1.5 million survey data points and 800,000 individual predictions into an accessible tool for indivuals across government, academia, and industry. The dashboard serves as a landing page for visitors to the site and includes links to existing tools in the BAM portfolio, for those curious.

![Dashboard High Level Overview](img/dashboard-mock-up-v1.png)


## Technical Requirements and Objectives

Improvements to current model website:
• Current website is static. The goal is to make this more interactive and dynamic to user input.
• Current website is targetted towards sppecialized individuals in the field, containing specialized jargon. The goal is to make this dashboard easy to use and understand for the general public.

![Existing version of BAM website](img/BAM-existing-website-visuals.png)

Audience
• Advanced users interacting with the model products using advanced statistical software.

2024 Survey
• Identified a need for more accessible interactive tools.

Conservation applications:
• Population and trend estimations.
• Conservation and land-use planning.
• Knowledge of habitat relationships.
• Investigating population decline.

“Tools need to be simple and effective for anyone to actually use them.”



## Exploratory Data Analysis
### Basic EDA

The main data scope for the dashboard included model outputs only. The files were stored in the cloud and included the following:

- Raster files - The main output of the model  containing 3 bands: mean density, standard deviation of density, and the mean distance of species detection.
- Excel files - Static metadata associated with the bird species, region, and importance of the features, saved in xlsx format.
- CSV files - The input to the model, including raw observations binned by Lat and Lon. Used to distinguish between different methods of bird spotting.

For EDA purposes, we narrowed down the scope to a single species of bird - **Canada Warbler**.

Initial EDA involved plotting the different color bands in the TIF. This was helpful for investigating what to expect from the TIF files. We clarified that the bands corresponded to the following:

- First band: Mean density of the bird population.
- Second band: Standard Deviation of the Mean population density.
- Third band: Mean distance of observations from the detection apparatus.

![Split of Raster Data into Bands](img/raster-bands-split.png)


### Data Concerns and Constraints

Potential issues we found with the data include:

- Finely detailed raster: The native TIF rasters are ~800K predictions per TIF output. This entire high-res output is unnecessarily loaded for every interactive adjustment; even when zooming into a specific region. 

- CRS adjustment: The TIFs arrive formatted with the EPSG:3978 (Statistics Canada Lambert) Coordinate Reference System. This does not allow for caching and every render triggers a full coordinate transformation pass. 

- Eager Loading of data: Every filter adjustment triggers a full TIF decode, CRS transformation, and PNG/COG encoding. This bogs down the UI resulting in a laggy and  inefficient user experience.

To address this, we are exploring options using the Cloud-Optimized GeoTIF format (COG). COG is analogous to Parquet for tabular data; uses internal tiling + HTTP range requests to only render what is being displayed. 

We are also investigating alternate Lazy-Loading options within Shiny for R.


## Proposed Solutions
### UI/UX

The design brief provided by the client specified that the new dashboard should achieve the following:

- Maintains Existing Figures and Visuals
- Interactive
- Easy to Use
- Should accomodate future model versions

With this in mind, we propose the following UI design.

![Features of the dashboard highlighted](img/dashboard-features-hghlighted.png)

In keeping with the "minimalism" theme, we have limited the number of filters and visuals to only the most important. Additional visuals or fitlers are available to the public under an `advanced features` tab.

The visuals are now interactive and dynamic, changing based on user input.

Visuals used to be condensed down into one page, requiring users to scroll to view all the information relating to the dashboard. This was determined to be inefficient for the client, and so we have split each visual onto its own page.

A key consideration provided by the client was the ability to view legacy models in the dashboard. This was for reproducibility, and validation of performance (since they have thoroughly vetted outputs from earlier models). To address this, we have created a separate tab in the header allowing the user to view these past visualizations if needed.

Links to other BAM data products can be found in the `Model Access` tab.


### Data Pipeline

The main pipeline for the dashboard looks like this.

![Main data pipeline for the dashboard](img/main-data-pipeline.png)

Here is a breakdown of what the individual components of the pipeine look like.

#### Data Input

There are 2 data streams which we're ingesting into the dashboard- outputs from the model and static metadata. The model output consists of the different TIF files, one for each species, location, and year in the dataset. The metadata includes information relating to the bird species (including English names, lating names, genus etc.), location, and region. These will primarily feed into the main filters of the dashboard.

Currently, these files are stored on a Google Drive. To improve loading speeds, we suggest preloading these model outputs to a SQL (or equivalent) database. This will enable fast retrieval and structured data validation.



#### Preprocessing

Preprocessing is minimal for both data streams. Since we're dealing with discrete model outputs, data format can be safely assumed to be standardized. 

Before loading the data to the app, we perform some data validation checks on the metadata:

- Column name validation,
- Data type validation,
- NaN management.

Preprocessing that needs to be done on the TIF file includes:

- Color band extraction,
- CRS validation,
- Conversion to Cloud Optimized Geotiff (COG)

Our plan is to run these preprocessing steps in advance such that it is ready to be ingested into the dashboard at runtime.


#### App

The app consists of a server and UI. In this section we talk about the data incorporated by the server.

Basic render targets include:

- Bird Metadata: A static table of metadata that can be sliced by filters
- Filters: The filters incorporate metadata from the excel file. These serve as lookup tables for the dashboard and will feed into the main filters / slicers in the UI.
- Main Raster: Simple loading of the different raster bands to memory.
- Metrics: Extraction of metrics (mean, sd of abundance, distance from detectors), from raster file.

There is an opportunity for further data metrics derived from these base ones. This shall be explored at a later data and in consulatation with the client.


#### Front End

The front end of the dashboard includes the follwing visuals, at bare minimum.

An interactive map that follows the following format.

![Dashboard map visual](img/dashboard-visuals-map.png)

A bar chart showing the population densities by region.

![Dashboard bar chart visual](img/dashboard-visuals-bar-chart.png)

A static table containing raw numeric metrics as seen below.

![Dashboard static tables](img/dashboard-visuals-static-table.png)

There is an opportunity for additional visuals, based on client feedback and consultation. These will be addressed after the basic structure of the dashboard has been implemented.


## Conclusion





## Links / References
